---
title: "01 — Read an image"
subtitle: "Load a NIfTI file and get your data — with the spatial frame still attached."
jupyter: python3
sidebar: start
---

Reading an image is one function call. You get the array you expect, plus the spatial frame that came with the file — so later steps can't quietly mix images that don't line up.

## Load a file

```{python}
from pathlib import Path
import sys

sys.path.insert(0, str(Path.cwd().parents[1] / "src"))

import numpy as np
import neuroim as ni

repo = Path.cwd().parents[1] if Path.cwd().name == "start" else Path.cwd()

bold = ni.read_image(str(repo / "golden_tests/fixtures/tiny_bold.nii.gz"))
arr = np.asarray(bold.data)

bold.shape, arr.dtype, float(arr.mean())
```

That's your data: a 4-D array, its dtype, and a value you can sanity-check. Indexing works the way you'd expect — `bold.data[..., 0]` is the first volume, `bold.data[x, y, z, :]` is one voxel's time series.

Read a 3-D mask the same way:

```{python}
mask = ni.read_image(str(repo / "golden_tests/fixtures/tiny_mask.nii.gz"), type="vol")
mask.shape, int(np.asarray(mask.data).sum())
```

The one extra thing each object carries is `.space` — the spatial frame (dimensions, voxel sizes, orientation) read from the file. It travels with the data automatically; you never assemble it by hand. The [next page](02-space.qmd) is about what that frame does for you.

## Which reader runs

`read_image` picked `DenseNeuroVec` for the 4-D BOLD (the trailing axis is time) and, with `type="vol"`, `DenseNeuroVol` for the 3-D mask. `type=` chooses the *reader*, it does not reinterpret bytes:

- `type="auto"` (default) — pick by dimensionality.
- `type="vol"` — volume reader; on a 4-D file it takes volume 0.
- `type="vec"` — vector reader; on a 3-D file it promotes to a singleton-time 4-D `(…, 1)`.

For most workflows the default is what you want; pass `type=` only when you need a specific shape.

## Why a typed object?

The return type is not bookkeeping — it's what lets you write analysis code without defensive checks. A `DenseNeuroVec` is the 4-D, time-aware type, so its time methods are just there:

```{python}
bold.temporal_snr  # a time-aware method, available because this is a NeuroVec
```

You call `bold.temporal_snr(mask=mask)` directly — no `if data.ndim == 4:` guard, no passing the affine around by hand. If you call a time method on a 3-D volume, that's a wrong call your editor and a type checker can catch, instead of a confusing array error deep in a reduction. The safety is a side effect of getting the right object back; you don't have to think about it to benefit from it.

## What it refuses

You can skip this on a first read — it's here so you know the edges.

- **Unknown reader mode.** `type=` accepts only `"auto"`, `"vol"`, or `"vec"`. Anything else raises `ValueError: type must be one of 'auto', 'vol', or 'vec'` rather than guessing.
- **A bare array as the return.** `read_image` always returns a typed object with `.space` attached; it never hands back a plain `numpy.ndarray`. When you explicitly want the raw array, `bold.data[...]` works for any image, `vol.as_array()` is available on volume objects, and `neuroim.compat.as_array(obj)` is the generic helper — opting out is deliberate, never accidental.

`read_image` is the entry point. It uses `nibabel` underneath, but that's an implementation detail. If you already hold a nibabel image for some other reason, `ni.from_nibabel(img)` is the explicit bridge — see [interop](../cookbook/interop-with-nibabel.qmd).

## Next

[02 — Spatial frames](02-space.qmd) introduces `NeuroSpace`, the frame that travels with every image and quietly keeps later steps from mixing mismatched data.
